perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Initial Term. This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and, unless sooner terminated pursuant to Sections 3.1 [Advertising and Promotion] or 10.2 [Termination for Breach], shall remain effective for two (2) years from and after the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). This agreement shall automatically renew for additional successive terms of twelve (12) months each at the end of the Initial Term ("Renewal Terms"), unless either party notifies the other in writing at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the Initial Term. As used in this agreement, "Term" shall mean the Initial Term and the Renewal Terms, if any.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Initial Term. This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and, unless sooner terminated pursuant to Sections 3.1 [Advertising and Promotion] or 10.2 [Termination for Breach], shall remain effective for two (2) years from and after the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). This agreement may renew for additional successive terms of twelve (12) months each at the end of the Initial Term ("Renewal Terms"), subject to either party notifying the other in writing at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the Initial Term. As used in this agreement, "Term" shall mean the Initial Term and the Renewal Terms, if any.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall automatically renew" to "may renew", we introduce uncertainty regarding the renewal of the agreement. The original text suggests automatic renewal unless notice is given, while the modified text suggests renewal is contingent upon notice, regardless of whether it's a renewal or a non-renewal notice. This contradicts the intent of the original automatic renewal clause, creating uncertainty about the contract's term and requirements for renewal.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.1 Initial Term. This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and, unless sooner terminated pursuant to Sections 3.1 [Advertising and Promotion] or 10.2 [Termination for Breach], shall remain effective for two (2) years from and after the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). This agreement may renew for additional successive terms of twelve (12) months each at the end of the Initial Term ("Renewal Terms"), subject to either party notifying the other in writing at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the Initial Term. As used in this agreement, "Term" shall mean the Initial Term and the Renewal Terms, if any.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall automatically renew" to "may renew" introduces a direct contradiction. The original language created an expectation of automatic renewal unless a specific action (notice of non-renewal) was taken. The revised language makes renewal contingent upon some further action or agreement, creating uncertainty and potential conflict regarding the contract's term. This directly contradicts the previously established understanding of how the contract term is extended, therefore rendering the contradicted section incompatible with the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 During the Term, (a) Women.com will not buy, sell, display, distribute advertising from (including, but not limited to, banner ads, buttons, badges, text links, hyperlinks or editorial mentions) or otherwise promote a Competitive Company on any page of the Diet Center; (b) eDiets will be the exclusive third party provider of content and interactive tools for diets and diet counseling to Women.com within the Diet Center; (c) Women.com will not enter into a relationship with a Competitive Company that involves any of the following; (i) custom developed mini or micro web sites; (ii) content integration; (iii) discounts offered only to Women.com members on the Member Central page of the Women.com Sites; (iv) editorial endorsement (such as Source: EDIETS COM INC, 10QSB, 10/30/2000 "Brought to you by....") or fixed placement of 468x60 (top) and 125x365 (side) banners within eight (8) of the Content Features selected by eDiets; and (v) anchor sponsorship in any multi-sponsor Women.com promotion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Women.com shall be entitled to (aa) provide mini and micro web sites and Women.com promotions and sponsorships to Jenny Craig; (bb) sell, display, or distribute advertisements, including banner advertisements, newsletter placements, internet radio, commerce placements and market research, or otherwise promote Competitive Companies on pages other than the Gateway Page of the Diet Center.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 During the Term, (a) Women.com will not buy, sell, display, distribute advertising from (including, but not limited to, banner ads, buttons, badges, text links, hyperlinks or editorial mentions) or otherwise promote a Competitive Company on any page of the Diet Center, with the exception of Jenny Craig; (b) eDiets will be the exclusive third party provider of content and interactive tools for diets and diet counseling to Women.com within the Diet Center; (c) Women.com will not enter into a relationship with a Competitive Company that involves any of the following; (i) custom developed mini or micro web sites; (ii) content integration; (iii) discounts offered only to Women.com members on the Member Central page of the Women.com Sites; (iv) editorial endorsement (such as Source: EDIETS COM INC, 10QSB, 10/30/2000 "Brought to you by....") or fixed placement of 468x60 (top) and 125x365 (side) banners within eight (8) of the Content Features selected by eDiets; and (v) anchor sponsorship in any multi-sponsor Women.com promotion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Women.com shall be entitled to (aa) provide mini and micro web sites and Women.com promotions and sponsorships to Jenny Craig; (bb) sell, display, or distribute advertisements, including banner advertisements, newsletter placements, internet radio, commerce placements and market research, or otherwise promote Competitive Companies on pages other than the Gateway Page of the Diet Center.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "with the exception of Jenny Craig" to clause (a) while retaining the "Notwithstanding the foregoing, Women.com shall be entitled to (aa) provide mini and micro web sites and Women.com promotions and sponsorships to Jenny Craig," an in-text contradiction is created. The first statement suggests that Jenny Craig is an exception to the general exclusivity rule within the Diet Center, while the second reiterates that Women.com is entitled to provide mini-sites and promotions specifically for Jenny Craig. This creates confusion as to whether Women.com is generally allowed to promote Jenny Craig within the Diet Center, or only through mini-sites and promotions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 During the Term, (a) Women.com will not buy, sell, display, distribute advertising from (including, but not limited to, banner ads, buttons, badges, text links, hyperlinks or editorial mentions) or otherwise promote a Competitive Company on any page of the Diet Center, with the exception of Jenny Craig; (b) eDiets will be the exclusive third party provider of content and interactive tools for diets and diet counseling to Women.com within the Diet Center; (c) Women.com will not enter into a relationship with a Competitive Company that involves any of the following; (i) custom developed mini or micro web sites; (ii) content integration; (iii) discounts offered only to Women.com members on the Member Central page of the Women.com Sites; (iv) editorial endorsement (such as Source: EDIETS COM INC, 10QSB, 10/30/2000 "Brought to you by....") or fixed placement of 468x60 (top) and 125x365 (side) banners within eight (8) of the Content Features selected by eDiets; and (v) anchor sponsorship in any multi-sponsor Women.com promotion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Women.com shall be entitled to (aa) provide mini and micro web sites and Women.com promotions and sponsorships to Jenny Craig; (bb) sell, display, or distribute advertisements, including banner advertisements, newsletter placements, internet radio, commerce placements and market research, or otherwise promote Competitive Companies on pages other than the Gateway Page of the Diet Center.
perturbation.justification: The addition of "with the exception of Jenny Craig" in clause (a) creates a strong in-text contradiction with the subsequent "Notwithstanding the foregoing, Women.com shall be entitled to (aa) provide mini and micro web sites and Women.com promotions and sponsorships to Jenny Craig." The first clause implies a general exception for Jenny Craig across all advertising, while the second clause limits the permissible promotion of Jenny Craig to specific activities (mini-sites, promotions, sponsorships). This leads to confusion and a direct conflict regarding the scope of allowed Jenny Craig promotions, resulting in a structural flaw that makes the contract inconsistent and uncertain, and creates ambiguity regarding Women.com's obligations. As such, there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 The parties acknowledge that, in the course of performing duties under this Agreement, each party may obtain Confidential Information from the other party. For the purposes of this Section 11, the party disclosing Confidential Information shall be the "Disclosing Party" and the party receiving Confidential Information shall be the "Receiving Party." "Confidential Information" means any and all technical and non-technical information provided by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party and shall include, but not be limited to, all information regarding (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secrets, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, samples, media, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of the Company, and including, without limitation, the Company's information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the Company provides regarding third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 The parties acknowledge that, in the course of performing duties under this Agreement, each party may obtain Confidential Information from the other party. For the purposes of this Section 11, the party disclosing Confidential Information shall be the "Disclosing Party" and the party receiving Confidential Information shall be the "Receiving Party." "Confidential Information" means any and all technical and non-technical information provided by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party and shall include solely information regarding (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secrets, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, samples, media, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of the Company, and including, without limitation, the Company's information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the Company provides regarding third parties.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "any and all" to "solely" a very restrictive interpretation has been made, while at the same time, there is still a list of information which by its nature includes any and all kind of information. Which contradicts the the first statemetn. The modified definition of "Confidential Information" restricts it to the list, but the expansive nature of that specific list still encompasses "any and all technical and non-technical information". This introduces an in-text contradiction because the first part of the sentence states that Confidential Information includes 'solely' the listed items, but then proceeds to include an extensive list that covers virtually everything that was supposed to be limited by that word.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11.1 The parties acknowledge that, in the course of performing duties under this Agreement, each party may obtain Confidential Information from the other party. For the purposes of this Section 11, the party disclosing Confidential Information shall be the "Disclosing Party" and the party receiving Confidential Information shall be the "Receiving Party." "Confidential Information" means any and all technical and non-technical information provided by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party and shall include solely information regarding (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secrets, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, samples, media, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of the Company, and including, without limitation, the Company's information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the Company provides regarding third parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' by stating it includes 'solely' the listed items. However, the extensive list following 'solely' encompasses virtually all information, negating the restrictive nature of 'solely'. This creates a direct in-text contradiction with the original, unedited sentence and potentially with other clauses that assume a broader scope of what 'Confidential Information' covers. The use of "solely" is supposed to limit the definition, but the list that follows undermines that limitation, causing confusion.
